professor.name: Dr. Okafor
professor.text: We've been discussing light pollution this semester. A number of towns near observatories have adopted "dark-sky" ordinances that require shielded, warmer streetlights and switch off unnecessary lighting after midnight. Supporters say this protects nocturnal wildlife and cuts wasted energy, while critics worry that dimmer streets could leave residents feeling less safe at night. Should more communities adopt dark-sky lighting rules? Why or why not?

students.name: Claire
students.text: I think dark-sky rules are worth adopting. Excessive artificial light disrupts the migration and breeding patterns of nocturnal species, and much of that glare is simply wasted energy aimed uselessly at the sky. Properly shielded lamps still illuminate the pavement where people actually need it.

students.name: Paul
students.text: I see the ecological argument, but switching lights off after midnight worries me. Well-lit streets can deter crime and help people feel secure walking home late, and that sense of safety matters enormously to many residents. A blanket lighting curfew risks trading one legitimate concern for another.

professor.name: Dr. Reyes
professor.text: Urban planners have recently revived an old idea: the "fifteen-minute city," where groceries, schools, clinics, and parks all sit within a short walk or bike ride of home. Advocates argue this cuts car dependence and rebuilds local community, while skeptics counter that it's unrealistic in sprawling suburbs and could push up housing costs in the few walkable districts that already qualify. Do you think cities should redesign neighborhoods around this model?

students.name: Mia
students.text: I find the fifteen-minute model genuinely appealing. When daily errands are reachable on foot, people drive less, spend more time in their own neighborhoods, and gradually come to know their neighbors, which strengthens the social fabric. Reducing our dependence on cars carries obvious environmental benefits too.

students.name: Carlos
students.text: The vision sounds appealing, but I'm skeptical about the execution. Most suburbs were built entirely around the car, and retrofitting shops and clinics into them would be enormously expensive. There's also a real risk that the few walkable districts become so desirable that ordinary families can't afford to live there.

professor.name: Dr. Hassan
professor.text: We've been discussing how cities treat their industrial past this semester. Old factories, mills, and warehouses often sit abandoned, leaving communities with a choice: demolish them for new construction, or convert them into housing, museums, or markets. Some argue that preserving these structures keeps a neighborhood's history and character alive, while others say adaptive reuse is costly and that the same land could serve far more people if rebuilt from scratch. Which approach would you favor?

students.name: Aisha
students.text: I lean toward preserving and repurposing these buildings. A converted mill carries the memory of the work and people that shaped a place, and that tangible connection to the past is something a brand-new structure simply cannot replicate. Reusing what already stands is also considerably less wasteful than demolition.

students.name: Ryan
students.text: I appreciate the sentimental value, but nostalgia shouldn't override practical needs. Many old industrial buildings are structurally unsound or contaminated, and the cost of bringing them up to modern code can exceed building anew. Where a city faces a housing shortage, dense new construction may serve many more residents.

professor.name: Dr. Novak
professor.text: More and more restaurants have recently begun eliminating tipping and folding a fixed service charge into their menu prices instead. Owners who've made the switch say it produces steadier, fairer pay for kitchen and waitstaff alike, while critics argue that removing tips weakens the incentive for attentive service and that customers dislike seeing higher numbers on the menu. Do you think restaurants should abandon tipping in favor of service-included pricing?

students.name: Wendy
students.text: I think building service into the price is the fairer system. Tipping leaves workers' incomes at the mercy of customers' moods and biases, and it frequently shortchanges the kitchen staff who never meet a single diner. A predictable wage written into the menu treats the entire team more equitably.

students.name: Marco
students.text: There's something to that, but I'd miss the accountability tips create. When part of a server's pay hinges on the experience they deliver, they have a direct reason to stay attentive. Bundle it all into a flat charge and that link between effort and reward weakens, which could let quality slip.
